description:
You are the on–call operations engineer.  
    A small incident-response drill has been staged in your home directory.  
    Your goal is to build a tiny SQLite database from two CSV files, run an
    investigation query, and create a human-readable triage report.

    Step-by-step business requirements
    1.  Create a new directory called “/home/user/incidents”.
    2.  Inside that directory create a SQLite 3 database file
        named “incidents.db”.
    3.  In that database create two tables **exactly** as follows  
        (the column order and types must match so the grader can query them):
        • servers(  
             id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,  
             hostname TEXT NOT NULL,  
             status TEXT NOT NULL  
          )  
        • events(  
             id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,  
             server_id INTEGER NOT NULL,  
             timestamp TEXT NOT NULL,        -- ISO 8601 string  
             description TEXT NOT NULL,  
             FOREIGN KEY(server_id) REFERENCES servers(id)  
          )

    4.  Two CSV files have already been staged for you in  
        “/home/user/source_data/servers.csv” and
        “/home/user/source_data/events.csv”.  
        Import their contents (including **all** rows) into the appropriate
        tables. The files are comma-separated and each contains a header row.

    5.  After loading the data, run an investigation with this business logic:
        • A server is considered “impacted” if **either**
          a) its current status in the servers table is ‘down’, **or**  
          b) it has one or more related rows in the events table whose
             description contains the substring “down” **and**
             whose timestamp ≥ ‘2023-01-12 11:00:00’.
        • For every “impacted” server calculate:  
          – the total number of such “down” events (0 + is allowed)  
          – the most recent timestamp of those “down” events
            (use “N/A” if there are zero qualifying events)
        • Immediately update the servers table so that every “impacted”
          server’s status becomes the string literal ‘investigating’.

    6.  Produce a plain-text report file at
        “/home/user/incidents/triage_report.log”.
        For **each** impacted server write **exactly one** line using this
        pipe-delimited format (no leading/trailing spaces):

            <hostname>|<previous_status>|<down_event_count>|<last_down_timestamp>

        Notes on the fields:
        • <hostname>            – the host’s name as it appeared in servers.csv  
        • <previous_status>     – the status that was stored **before** you
                                   changed it to ‘investigating’  
        • <down_event_count>    – integer count defined above  
        • <last_down_timestamp> – most recent qualifying timestamp or “N/A”

        Example of a correctly formatted single line:

            cache-01|down|2|2023-01-12 11:05:00

        End the file with a single trailing newline; do **not** add any extra
        blank lines.

    7.  Do **not** print the report to stdout; only save it to the path shown
        above. You can of course print intermediary SQL results or debugging
        output to the terminal while you work.

    The automated tests will verify all of the following:
    • “/home/user/incidents/incidents.db” exists.  
    • The two tables exist with the correct schema and exactly the data that
      results from the CSV import plus your status updates.  
    • “/home/user/incidents/triage_report.log” exists and matches the format
      and content rules precisely.  
    • The status of each impacted server inside incidents.db has been changed
      to ‘investigating’.

    Take whatever intermediate steps you need (shell utilities, the sqlite3
    CLI, etc.) to satisfy all of the above requirements. Good luck!
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing files available when the scenario starts -->
    /home/user/source_data/servers.csv      (read-only, 3 rows + header)
    /home/user/source_data/events.csv       (read-only, 6 rows + header)

    # Contents of servers.csv
    id,hostname,status
    1,web-01,up
    2,db-01,up
    3,cache-01,down

    # Contents of events.csv
    id,server_id,timestamp,description
    101,1,2023-01-12 10:15:00,Deployment
    102,3,2023-01-12 10:16:00,Cache restart
    103,3,2023-01-12 11:00:00,Cache down
    104,3,2023-01-12 11:05:00,Cache still down
    105,2,2023-01-12 11:10:00,DB maintenance
    106,1,2023-01-12 12:00:00,Deployment complete

    ------------------------------------------------------------------------
    Expected final state AFTER the agent completes all tasks
    ------------------------------------------------------------------------

    1.  File “/home/user/incidents/triage_report.log” exists and contains
        EXACTLY the following single line plus newline:

        cache-01|down|2|2023-01-12 11:05:00\n

    2.  Database “/home/user/incidents/incidents.db”  
        • Table servers contains three rows.  
          Row breakdown:
              id | hostname | status
              1  | web-01   | up
              2  | db-01    | up
              3  | cache-01 | investigating      <-- updated from “down”
        • Table events contains the six rows imported verbatim from events.csv
          (no rows added or deleted).

    3.  Schema of each table matches exactly what is specified in the public
        task description (column names, order, types, PRIMARY KEY, and the
        FOREIGN KEY).

    The grading harness will:
        • launch sqlite3 against /home/user/incidents/incidents.db
        • check PRAGMA table_info for column definitions
        • perform SELECT queries to verify row counts & values
        • read and compare /home/user/incidents/triage_report.log byte-for-byte
